name: notable dark red mushrooms
shortDescription: Notable dark red mushrooms grow with a deep wine-colored cap that looks richer than most things found under rotten logs. Cooks use them cautiously, alchemists use them eagerly, and experienced foragers never confuse beauty with safety.
flavorText: “Dark red mushrooms make every basket look expensive and every supper slightly suspicious.”

name: notable flat purple mushroom
shortDescription: A notable flat purple mushroom spreads wide under shade, its cap smooth and low like a little violet saucer set for forest spirits. Its color draws buyers quickly, though its flavor and effects depend on who picked it and how honest they are.
flavorText: “Purple fungus is rarely humble, even when it grows in dirt.”

name: notable flat red mushroom
shortDescription: A notable flat red mushroom has a broad cap that sits close to the earth, bright enough to warn the careless and tempt the curious. Herbalists prize the dried slices, but only after someone else has done the identifying twice.
flavorText: “Red caps are nature’s way of making fools read labels.”

name: notable flat yellow mushroom
shortDescription: A notable flat yellow mushroom glows warmly among damp leaves, looking almost cheerful for something that grew in shadow. It is favored for bright dyes, earthy stews, and remedies that need a little sun-colored confidence.
flavorText: “Yellow mushrooms bring daylight to baskets that smell of rain.”

name: fabled pink mushroom
shortDescription: A fabled pink mushroom is rare enough that foragers lower their voices when describing where they found it. Its delicate color makes it look sweet, but the best buyers know rarity in the wild is usually protected by a few private dangers.
flavorText: “Pink mushrooms are proof the forest can blush while lying.”

name: scarce massive purple mushroom
shortDescription: A scarce massive purple mushroom is large enough to seem less harvested than negotiated with. Its thick cap and violet flesh bring good money from dyers, cooks, and alchemists who own knives wide enough to make useful portions.
flavorText: “A mushroom that size does not grow; it establishes itself.”

name: scarce spotted red mushroom
shortDescription: A scarce spotted red mushroom wears pale marks across its cap like a warning written by someone with decorative taste. It is admired by collectors, feared by cautious cooks, and watched closely by anyone who knows spots can mean more than charm.
flavorText: “The spots are not there for beauty, though beauty helps the sale.”

name: purple mushrooms
shortDescription: Purple mushrooms bring a dusky color to the forager’s basket, earthy in smell and dramatic in appearance. They are common enough for market trade but still strange enough that buyers ask whether they are food, dye, medicine, or trouble.
flavorText: “Purple mushrooms let ordinary soup pretend it met a wizard.”

name: red mushrooms
shortDescription: Red mushrooms are bright little flags in the leaf litter, easy to spot and hard to trust without a trained eye. Some are useful, some are delicious, and some are the reason old foragers laugh at brave young ones.
flavorText: “Red mushrooms do not hide their risk; people merely ignore the color.”

name: short purple mushrooms
shortDescription: Short purple mushrooms grow close to the ground in squat clusters, easy to miss until their color gathers under shade. They dry well, stain fingers faintly, and make apprentices ask why useful ingredients so often grow where knees get wet.
flavorText: “The forest keeps some treasures low so pride has to bend.”

name: short yellow mushrooms
shortDescription: Short yellow mushrooms are small, dense, and cheerful-looking, like drops of butter misplaced in moss. They are good trade for cooks and hedge-healers, provided the picker knows the difference between golden and regrettable.
flavorText: “A yellow cap can promise dinner or a difficult afternoon.”

name: skinny purple mushrooms
shortDescription: Skinny purple mushrooms rise on narrow stems with a theatrical little cap at the top, fragile but eye-catching. They are better dried than carried fresh, because a careless basket turns them from fine stock into violet crumbs.
flavorText: “Skinny mushrooms survive by being noticed before being stepped on.”

name: skinny red mushrooms
shortDescription: Skinny red mushrooms stand like tiny warning candles along damp roots and fallen branches. Their stems snap easily, but their color survives drying, making them popular with dye-makers and suspicious with cooks.
flavorText: “Thin red stems look harmless until the recipe asks for only one.”

name: skinny yellow mushrooms
shortDescription: Skinny yellow mushrooms are wiry little fungi with bright caps and a habit of growing in crowded, brittle bunches. They are light to carry but troublesome to sort, and the best ones smell faintly of wet straw and warm earth.
flavorText: “A basket of skinny yellows is mostly air, color, and careful handling.”

name: tiny purple mushrooms
shortDescription: Tiny purple mushrooms are almost too small for serious trade, but their color gives them a charm that sells by the pinch. Alchemists like their concentration, cooks like their novelty, and children like being told not to touch them.
flavorText: “Tiny mushrooms remind merchants that small things can still demand labels.”

name: tiny red mushrooms
shortDescription: Tiny red mushrooms scatter through moss like spilled beads, bright enough to tempt the eye and small enough to vanish in a careless palm. They are sold in little packets to people who trust precise measures more than appetite.
flavorText: “The smallest red cap can still change a whole pot.”

name: tiny yellow mushrooms
shortDescription: Tiny yellow mushrooms are delicate specks of gold in damp places, gathered slowly by patient foragers with sharp eyes. Their value comes less from bulk than from the trouble of collecting enough to matter.
flavorText: “Tiny yellow mushrooms are paid for in bent backs before coin.”

name: yellow mushrooms
shortDescription: Yellow mushrooms are bright, earthy market stock, useful for cooking, dyes, and mild remedies when properly known. They look friendly in a basket, which is precisely why good merchants label them clearly.
flavorText: “Yellow mushrooms smile in the stall; wise buyers still ask questions.”

name: dead leafs
shortDescription: Dead leafs are dry, brittle, and easy to dismiss until a healer, dyer, or charm-worker asks for the right kind. Autumn leaves carry color, tannin, scent, and the quiet usefulness of things no longer trying to live.
flavorText: “A dead leaf is not worthless; it is simply finished being a roof.”

name: green leafs
shortDescription: Green leafs are fresh plant stock for poultices, wrappings, stews, dyes, and a hundred small trades that begin with something living. Their worth fades quickly, so good sellers keep them cool, damp, and out of boastful sunlight.
flavorText: “Fresh leaves sell on the same clock that wilts them.”

name: pink leafs
shortDescription: Pink leafs are decorative, tender, and often more useful to perfumers and charmers than to cooks. They lend a soft color to sachets and festival bundles, making ordinary plant trade feel briefly like courtship.
flavorText: “Pink leaves make even herb bundles look like apologies.”

name: red leafs
shortDescription: Red leafs carry the color of late season, dried blood, and old bonfires depending on who is buying them. Dyers prize their stain, herbalists their bitterness, and storytellers their habit of making any bundle look dramatic.
flavorText: “Red leaves are autumn’s last loud argument.”

name: blue quils pads
shortDescription: Blue quils pads are strange aquatic leaves with a cool blue cast and ribbed surfaces that hold droplets like little lenses. Pond-keepers, ink-makers, and alchemists value them for their color and the faint mineral taste they lend to mixtures.
flavorText: “Some pond plants grow as if the water taught them handwriting.”

name: blue wilter
shortDescription: Blue wilter is a drooping plant with blue-toned leaves that look tired even when freshly cut. Its wilt is the point: healers use it in calming blends, and dyers prize the melancholy stain it leaves behind.
flavorText: “Blue wilter looks sad enough to be useful.”

name: blue stellos
shortDescription: Blue stellos grows in starry clusters, each small bloom or leaf catching light with a cool, clean brightness. It is popular in charm bundles, night gardens, and remedies sold to people who want medicine to look trustworthy.
flavorText: “Blue stellos is what the herb shelf uses for ceremony.”
